title: Dyeing with dock leaves
subtitle: A foraged dye producing soft yellow-green on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Broad-leaved dock (Rumex obtusifolius) is one of the most widely available foraged dye plants in the British Isles, found at roadsides and field margins throughout the year. It produces a soft, grassy yellow-green on alum-mordanted wool.
sourceNotes: Dock as a natural dye documented in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in historical foraging and plant-dye literature. Rumex species are widely described as dye plants in pre-industrial British dyeing records.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that bonds with both the wool fibre and the dye to fix the colour. Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is the standard mordant for most plant yellows.
The pot of extracted dye liquid into which the mordanted fibre is placed to take on colour.
A dye that fades over time with light or washing. Dock produces a moderately fugitive colour; it is stable in indoor, low-light use but fades in prolonged sunlight.

content.text: Dock leaves make a soft yellow-green
from a plant that most British gardens and field edges have in abundance. The colour is
in extended sunlight but holds well for textiles used indoors. A standard alum
shifts the colour toward a clearer yellow-green; an iron after-bath shifts it toward olive.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots and utensils are dedicated. Wear gloves and apron. Work outdoors or with ventilation. Alum is low-hazard; keep away from food.

items.name: Fresh broad-leaved dock leaves (Rumex obtusifolius)
items.qty: 400 g (roughly equal weight to the fibre)

content.text: Dissolve alum in hot water and add to the dye-pot with enough cold water to cover the fibre. Add the pre-wetted wool and raise to 80 degrees Celsius over 30 minutes. Hold for 45 minutes. Allow to cool in the bath before removing the fibre.

Chop or tear the dock leaves and place in a dye-pot with enough water to cover. Bring to a simmer and hold at 80 to 90 degrees Celsius for 45 minutes. Strain the liquid through a cloth or strainer into a clean dye-pot, discarding the plant material. The dye bath will be a warm yellow-green or greenish yellow.

Add the mordanted, still-damp fibre to the dye bath. Raise slowly to 80 degrees Celsius and hold for 45 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. Remove the fibre and rinse in progressively cooler water until the rinse water is clear.
The alum mordant bath and the dock dye bath can be composted or poured down the foul drain in small home quantities. Neither contains iron or copper.

Adding 2 g of dissolved iron sulphate to 2 litres of warm water as an after-bath shifts the yellow-green toward a darker, more muted olive. Immerse the rinsed fibre for 5 to 10 minutes, then rinse thoroughly. Dock roots produce a stronger tan-brown dye in the same process.

items.problem: The colour is very pale despite a full dye bath
items.cause: Dock is a moderate dye plant; the colour intensity depends heavily on how much leaf material is used relative to the fibre weight
items.fix: Use at least equal weight of dock to fibre (1:1 ratio) and simmer the plant material for the full 45 minutes before straining. Less plant material or shorter simmering time produces a paler result.

items.problem: The colour looked yellow-green in the pot but dried to a plain beige
items.cause: Under-mordanting: the alum was insufficient to bond the dye to the fibre
items.fix: Check the alum quantity: 15 g per 100 g fibre is the standard. Too little alum causes colour to wash out, leaving only a residual pale tan.
